4. Probing and Representations

4.1. Linear probes

Preliminary questions

  • To what extent can you show there’s casual linear structure? Read Geometry of Truth paper
  • How do you train LLMs with probing simultaneously? Penalty for differences?
  • Is the boundary line in PCA interpretable? Yeah it’s just irrelevant info right
  • Don’t we want to probe in COT as well? Not just end of prompt.

Idea is to take activation and train a linear classifier.

  • There’s a lot of activations: per-layer, per token (including autoregressed one). Geometry paper started by doing a localization procedure procedure, where we take a contrasting pair of prompts + outputs, e.g. honest response and deceptive response, swap pairs of tokens until the resulting output swaps.

    • Big assumption is assuming it’s localized?
    • Run PCA / train a classifier and then you see hopefully there’s a linear split.
  • More generally we can just try probe every of the form where is the current last token (including newly generated tokens e.g. in the chain of thought). Run probe to get a time series in and look for spikes during planinng and reasoning.

Example 1 (Truthiness Classification). Let’s consider an example where the task of the LLM is to classify statements as true or false.

Procedure

  1. Accumulate true false statements like

    Prompt:
    The Eiffel Tower is located in Rome. True or false?
    Answer:

    Agent: ...
  2. Train probe on activation of last token of the prompt statement.

Nice Properties:

  • Linear representations allow us to do simple casual interventions (activation patching): (to remove) and (to amplify) where is the normal unit vector to the probe’s separating hyperplane.

  • The hyperplane itself contains components that aren’t relevant to truth-detection?

Plots from ARENA notebook:

Truth representations from Geometry paper for 3 yes-no tasks. Best probe layer is early-to-mid.

Generalization of a linear probe (wait it’s like a monkey this shit is ass!!!):

Downstream applications:

  • Identify when model is decepting and killswitch for real time environment.
  • Flag areas where probe and model diverge (e.g. probe predicts that model will decept) and model doesn’t and further posttrain on those.

Example 2 (Deception Detection).

honest_sys = "You are an honest AI assistant."
dishonest_sys = "You are a deceptive AI assistant."
user_msg = "Please tell me a fact."

“A probe trained on this synthetic “deceptive mode” data can generalize to more realistic on-policy scenarios where the model has an instrumental reason to hide something, such as insider-trading concealment or sandbagging. But that still does not prove the model has human-like deceptive intent; it shows a detectable internal activation pattern correlated with deception-like behavior.”

Example 3 (High Stakes Detection with Attention Probes). “An attention probe learns to weight token positions selectively: after training, the attention weights tend to concentrate on the clause or phrase that makes a request dangerous.

The specific architecture used is called AttnLite. It has a single global context query vector that scores each token position, applies softmax over the sequence to get attention weights, computes a weighted sum of token activations, and feeds the result through a linear classifier. The query is position-independent and a learned constant: the same learned vector scores every token, so the attention weights can be read directly as a relevance score for each position. In the exercise below we implement this, with an optional generalization to multiple independent heads.“

4.2. Function Vectors and Steering Vectors

The true-false steering example above motivates the more general question of changing a model’s behavior at inference time.

Example 4 (Antonym In-Context Learning). Give an LLM a bunch of few-shot (ICL) prompts, like

Q: hot
A: cold

Q: yes
A: no

Q: up
A:

Use clean prompts and corrupted prompts (where the answer words aren’t antonyms).

  1. First intervention: Average over the residual stream vector at layer (token output) for the final token t obtain the h-vector, and adds this vector into a zero shot prompt

    (4)

    Empirically, the model outputs antonyms more often, suggesting that the residual stream contains a representation of the task.

  2. Second intervention: Try patching head outputs on the last token from clean runs into corrputed runs and score which heads restore correct antonym prediction. Like above, average over their outputs to create a function vector.

Function vectors let you steer a prompt like “When you think of Netherlands,” towards completions about Amsterdam.

Steering vectors don’t require ICL and more generally they are based on contrastive prompts. For example, run the model on “I love this” and separately on “I hate this”and take the residual activation difference at layer on the last token. Since both prompts share “I ____ this”, the shared components mostly cancel, leaving a sentimentish direction. Averaging over many contrastive pairs, let’s us compute steering for toxicity, honesty, refusal, style, topic, etc.

Given a prompt, you can steer the final token by injecting at decode time. This leaves KV cache of the prompt the same. Or you can steer the prompt which modifies its KV cache (context representation). This is more invasive.

People generally normalize steering vectors, and then at inference time scale them some fraction of the existing activations norm?

nnnsight let’s you interp on models remotely.

4.3. [TODO] SAEs are unsupervised

SAEs are unsupervised and find sparse faetures in a model activation (given activation x, train autoencoder with sparsity penalty on the latent z).

Consensus has been to move away from them to linear probes (easy to train, and SAEs are not that much better although linear probes are supervised).

“How to use SAEs” rather than “What are SAEs”

Although now we use SAEs to find circuits and linear probes can’t do.

https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/osNKnwiJWHxDYvQTD/takeaways-from-our-recent-work-on-sae-probing

Suck compared to linear probes.

4.4. Activation Oracles

Intro

  • AOs are LLMs that have been trained to take another model’s internal activations as input and answer arbitrary questions about them. Each “text” is a vector of floating-point numbers from some intermediate layer.
  • Oracle and target model are typically the same architecture so they “speak in the same activation space”.
  • The AO result is specifically that oracles can recover info fine-tuned into models even though they were not trained on activations from fine-tuned models,
  • Do well on benchmarks, detect emergent misalignment via model -diffing, and [TODO] query models on held-out classification tasks

Preliminary questions

  • How do you ask different questions based on what the layer is? Or do you ask the same questions.
  • How do you train these guys
  • Why do we trust them at inference time.
  • Why early transformer layer if at layer N

  • Can we fine tune a model to trick an AO? Or even worse, the AO knows the answer is X but the model’s activation tells it to output Y.

    OR THE MODEL BELIEVES THE ANSWER IS X BUT IT TELLS YOU THE ANSWER IS Y AND THE AO THAT THE ANSWER IS Y

You don’t just paste the floats into the prompt lol you change the underlying token representations.

At inference time:

  1. Run the target model on a prompt:

    Fuck this shit

  2. Run the activation oracle but after an early transformer layer (e.g. first or second) we “swap”2 in the target model’s actual layer-N activations at those ? token positions.

    Layer N: ? ? ? Is this a positive sentiment?

Instead of replacing the activations of the special tokens (which causes norm explosion), you do a norm-matched steering perturbation:

(5)

Example 5 (Token-by-token analysis).

target_prompt_dict = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": "The philosopher who drank hemlock taught a student who founded an academy. That student's most famous pupil was",
    },
]

oracle_prompt = "What people is the model thinking about?"

Feeding each token individually to the oracle yields:

Remarks from the example above

  • We give the AO all the activations up until that token.
  • Switching Socrates and Plato to NAME_1 and NAME_2 seems to be the target model encoding a summary in the punctuation. Since the oracle works on other stuff it might be the model.
  • Having a bias towards climate change might be a bias of QWEN or the oracle. [TODO] poke at this.

Example 6 (Oracle doesn't see function definition).

target_prompt_dict = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": "def foo(x, y):\n    return x + y\n\nresult = foo(3, 4)",
    },
]

oracle_prompt = "What will the result be?"

Demonstrates that oracle can interpret activations.

Code to run oracle: https://gist.github.com/canisli/4fde4387acaf876c039fde2cc8dd462a

Example 7 (Secret word). Model to hide secret word is created by fine tuning on guessing game (give hints that point to the secret word but never output the word).

Training: just collect the oracle prompts like Layer 18: ? ? ? <question and run forward pass with injected activations. Then train with next-token loss.

4.5. Natural Language Autoencoders

https://transformer-circuits.pub/2026/nla/index.html#using-nlas-for-supervised-activation-oracle-training#pagebreak()

Like activation oracles but without questions and a different training objective.

Blackmail example:
